name: Micro-Interactions — Engineering & Library Choices
definition_origin: Term coined by Dan Saffer in 'Microinteractions: Designing with Details' (O'Reilly, 2013, fully updated full-color edition 2014 with Don Norman foreword). Saffer formalized the trigger → rules → feedback → modes/loops structure. Reinforced by Val Head's 'Designing Interface Animation' (Rosenfeld 2016) and Pasquale D'Silva's seminal Medium essay 'Transitional Interfaces' (2013) which established that motion is information, not decoration.
core_mechanism: A micro-interaction is a single contained moment that does one job: communicate state change, confirm input, or reward action. Psychologically it works because (a) human perception expects continuity — abrupt state change triggers an attention spike that costs cognitive effort, while smooth motion preserves the object-permanence narrative; (b) anticipation-resolution loops are the same dopamine machinery that powers slot-machine engagement (Schultz 1997, dopamine prediction error) — a 200-400ms easing curve creates a tiny anticipation/payoff cycle; (c) tactile-style feedback (haptic + visual + audio) collapses ambiguity about whether an input registered, reducing the 'did it work?' anxiety that produces double-taps and rage-taps. The dual purpose is functional (system status) AND emotional (a tiny dopamine spike that says 'that felt good').
neuroscience_correlate: Mesolimbic dopamine release on prediction-resolved feedback (VTA → nucleus accumbens). Cerebellum predicts and tracks motion smoothness — non-physical motion (linear easing, sub-60fps jank) registers as a prediction error and feels 'wrong.' Mirror-neuron-style empathy with object motion (when a card spring-bounces back, users feel the squish).
empirical_evidence: Google's RAIL performance model (2015) established 100ms as the threshold for an action feeling instant; >300ms feels laggy. Apple's WWDC 'Designing Fluid Interfaces' talk (2018) demonstrated that 60fps is the floor and 120fps (ProMotion) is perceptibly smoother. A Nielsen Norman Group study on animated transitions found users rated interfaces with appropriate transitions as 30-40% 'easier to understand' than abrupt-cut counterparts [uncertain on exact figure]. Material 3 Expressive's 18,000-participant study (Google 2024) found expressive-motion variants increased perceived usability by ~32% and lifted task-completion confidence vs. flat baseline. Framer Motion's spring physics defaults (stiffness 100, damping 10, mass 1) produce a critically-damped feel close to natural object movement; production teams typically tune to stiffness 300, damping 30 for snappier UI.
measurable_outcome_metric: Frame-rate stability (P95 frame time <16.67ms for 60fps target, <8.33ms for 120fps); INP (Interaction-to-Next-Paint) <200ms for web; perceived-quality NPS delta; rage-tap rate (lower = better); session length and re-engagement on touched flows; A/B win-rate on conversion when adding meaningful motion.
engineering_tactics: FRAMER MOTION (web/React): use variants for declarative state-driven motion. Spring defaults (stiffness 100, damping 10, mass 1) work for most cases; for snappier UI use {type: 'spring', stiffness: 300, damping: 30, mass: 0.8}. For deterministic timing, use {type: 'tween', duration: 0.25, ease: [0.4, 0, 0.2, 1]} (Material standard). Variants pattern: const cardVariants = { rest: { scale: 1, y: 0 }, hover: { scale: 1.02, y: -2 }, press: { scale: 0.98, y: 0 } }; <motion.div variants={cardVariants} initial='rest' whileHover='hover' whileTap='press' />. Use layout prop for FLIP animations on list reorder, AnimatePresence for enter/exit. REACT NATIVE REANIMATED 3 (mobile): worklets run on UI thread, never JS. Pattern: const offset = useSharedValue(0); const animatedStyle = useAnimatedStyle(() => ({ transform: [{ translateY: withSpring(offset.value, { damping: 15, stiffness: 150 }) }] })); — withSpring for physical feel, withTiming for deterministic, withDecay for momentum. Use runOnJS only at boundaries. LOTTIE: import LottieView from 'lottie-react-native'; <LottieView source={require('./success.json')} autoPlay loop={false} renderMode='HARDWARE' speed={1.0} />; keep JSON <50KB, use .lottie format for 80% size reduction; control playback with ref.current.play(start, end) for milestone moments. RIVE: use state machines for interactive characters (login mascot eyes-follow-cursor, password-toggle eyes-cover); inputs are bool/trigger/number; one .riv file replaces dozens of Lottie variants because state transitions are authored, not chained from code. APPLE sensoryFeedback (iOS 17+, SwiftUI native): .sensoryFeedback(.success, trigger: viewModel.completedCount) — declarative, automatically respects user accessibility settings. EASING DISCIPLINE: never use linear (linear feels broken — physical objects accelerate). Standard tokens: ease-out [0, 0, 0.2, 1] for entering, ease-in [0.4, 0, 1, 1] for exiting, standard [0.4, 0, 0.2, 1] for moving on screen, emphasized [0.2, 0, 0, 1] (M3) for hero moments. Durations: 100ms (state toggle), 200-300ms (UI element movement), 400-500ms (page-level), 600ms+ feels slow. PERFORMANCE: animate only transform and opacity (compositor-only, no layout/paint); use will-change sparingly; in RN use useNativeDriver: true (or default with Reanimated 3); profile with React DevTools Profiler / Flipper / Xcode Instruments.
real_world_examples: Apple Stocks app price-flash green/red on update — 200ms ease-out background fade gives instant state read. Linear's command-bar entry — opens with combined fade+scale (0.96 → 1) on a 150ms ease-out, exits 100ms — the snappy timing IS the brand. Stripe's checkout 'card decoration' that morphs Visa/MC/Amex logos on number entry — Lottie-driven, 60fps. Duolingo lesson-complete screen at ~0.7s celebration delay: confetti particles, owl bounce, XP counter rolls up over 800ms with ease-out, all sequenced with audio-haptic cue. Robinhood candle-chart scrub — Reanimated worklet drives 120fps haptic + line-redraw on iPhone Pro. Apple Pay flat-card-to-3D-tilt on FaceID prompt — UIKit's UIViewPropertyAnimator with cubic-bezier(0.42, 0, 0.58, 1). Notion 'New Page' button: subtle scale 1 → 0.97 on press with spring back (stiffness 400 damping 25) — barely visible but feels alive vs. dead button. Headspace breathing animation — pure CSS scale 1 → 1.4 over 4s with ease-in-out, then hold 2s, ease-in-out back; the easing IS the meditation.
mobile_vs_web_diffs: Mobile: animations run on the UI thread via Reanimated worklets or native UIView/Compose; touch-down → animation start latency must be <16ms or feels broken; haptic pairing is table-stakes (every micro-interaction should consider .selection or .impact). Pixel-density and refresh rate vary (60Hz to 120Hz ProMotion / 144Hz Android) — design for the floor (60fps) but reward 120Hz devices. Web: GPU compositing via transform/opacity only; hover states drive a different micro-interaction class (entirely absent on mobile); no built-in haptics outside iOS Safari's Vibration API.
platform_design_language_alignment: Strongly aligns with both. iOS 26 Liquid Glass uses spring physics for every modal/sheet transition (the platform expects bouncy, physical motion). Material 3 Expressive (Android 16+) explicitly emphasizes expressive motion with new emphasized-easing tokens and ~30% larger motion amplitudes than M3 baseline. Building micro-interactions with spring physics + emphasized easing aligns naturally with both platforms.
claude_design_application: Specify libraries, easing tokens, and durations explicitly. Example prompt: 'Build a habit-card press interaction in React Native with Reanimated 3. On press-in: scale to 0.97 with withSpring({damping: 18, stiffness: 350}) and trigger Haptics.selectionAsync(). On release: spring back to 1.0. Use useAnimatedStyle and worklets — no JS-thread animations. Add a celebratory variant on completion: scale 1 → 1.08 → 1, opacity flash to 0.9 background, sequence over 600ms.' For web/Framer Motion: 'Build a button with three variants: rest, hover (y: -2, scale: 1.02), tap (scale: 0.97). Use spring {type: spring, stiffness: 300, damping: 25, mass: 0.8}. AnimatePresence the success checkmark Lottie when isSuccess=true.' Always force the easing token rather than letting Claude default.
stitch_application: Stitch can't simulate motion, so describe the moment in elaborate prose as if narrating film. Example fragment: 'When user taps the streak badge, the badge scales from 1.0 to 1.12 over 240ms with a soft overshoot (easing: cubic-bezier 0.34, 1.56, 0.64, 1), the gold gradient inside ripples outward, three small star particles emit from the center traveling 40px outward fading to 0 opacity over 400ms, and the streak number above counts up using a digit-rolodex effect lasting 600ms. The whole moment is single-tap-once feedback — celebratory but contained, not cartoonish.' Always specify (a) the trigger, (b) durations in ms, (c) easing curve in name OR cubic-bezier values, (d) what visually changes on which property, (e) what gets layered (haptic + audio cues even though Stitch can't render them — note them as design intent so devs honor them). For Items 16-17 specifically: Stitch CANNOT simulate motion or haptics, so commit to prose-narration of every animation moment.
ai_codegen_failure_mode: (1) Linear easing — Claude defaults to ease or linear when no curve specified, killing the physical feel. Counter: 'Use cubic-bezier(0.4, 0, 0.2, 1) for movement and cubic-bezier(0.34, 1.56, 0.64, 1) for celebratory overshoot. Forbid linear and ease.' (2) Spring damping at 1.0 (critically damped, no bounce, feels dead). Counter: 'damping: 18, stiffness: 350' for snappy UI; never default. (3) AI animates layout-triggering properties like width/height/top/left instead of transform/opacity, killing 60fps. Counter: 'Animate only transform and opacity. Never animate width/height/top/left.' (4) AI omits useNativeDriver in legacy Animated API, or runs worklets on JS thread. Counter: 'Use Reanimated 3 worklets exclusively. No Animated API. No runOnJS in animation paths.' (5) AI loops Lottie animations indefinitely (loop=true) when they should be milestone-only — kills perceived value and battery. Counter: 'Loop=false on success/celebration; only loop on indefinite-state indicators (loading).' (6) AI forgets to pair animation with haptic — visually celebrated but tactically silent. Counter: 'Every celebratory motion pairs with Haptics.notificationAsync(Success).'
anti_patterns: Animation everywhere — every button bounces, every fade-in stalls real work. 'Decorative' animation that delays action (a 600ms enter on a button users want to tap NOW). Looping Lottie set as background eating 30% CPU. Easing curves that overshoot on ALL motion (the brand becomes 'bouncy' rather than purposeful). Animating layout properties (width/height) on the JS thread → 12fps jank. Skipping prefers-reduced-motion / UIAccessibility.isReduceMotionEnabled checks → vestibular disorders, EU accessibility violations. AI-slop: chained-keyframe-soup with linear easing and no purpose ('vibe-coded' particle explosions on Save).
regulatory_risk: Low for the motion itself, but: prefers-reduced-motion is required by WCAG 2.1 AAA and EU EN 301 549 (mandatory for public-sector apps from 2025). Variable-reward celebration loops (Duolingo-style) on streaks/levels can attract attention under DSA addictive-design provisions and the UK Age-Appropriate Design Code if targeted at minors. State 'Low' for general use, 'Medium' for child-directed apps with celebratory loops.
pitfalls_caveats: Performance: Lottie large files (>200KB) tank low-end Android. Battery: continuous animations + GPU compositing drain ~5-15% extra per hour of active use. Accessibility: ALWAYS check reduce-motion settings (UIAccessibility.isReduceMotionEnabled / matchMedia('(prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)')) and disable non-essential motion. Cognitive load: too many simultaneous micro-interactions create visual noise. Cross-platform parity: spring physics tuning differs between Reanimated and Framer Motion — tune per platform.
cross_cultural_validity: Motion language is largely universal (gravity, momentum, easing perceived consistently across cultures). However, celebratory amplitude varies: Japanese/Korean markets respond well to subtler 'kawaii' overshoot, while Brazilian/Indian markets tolerate larger gestures. RTL: mirror directional motion (drawer slides from right in Arabic locale). Disability prevalence varies by region — vestibular sensitivity affects ~5% globally, so reduce-motion is a global requirement, not a Western nice-to-have.
